title: Harvard President Faust Links History and Leadership
summary: For nearly ten years, Harvard's President Drew Gilpin Faust has led the university with a historian's perspective. On June 30, she joined the Belfer Center's Applied History Project Faculty Working Group and shared her views on applying history to current situations.
body: In Harvard’s traditional Morning Prayers ushering in the current academic year, Harvard President Drew Gilpin Faust reflected on “a curious question” posed to her in 2007 after her appointment as president was announced: What did being a historian have to do with being president of Harvard? Faust recalled being surprised by the question “not just because historians have been successful presidents of lots of things, including Harvard—but because studying history seemed to me to be in many ways the ideal training for leadership.” This was the same message she carried on January 30 when she joined the Belfer Center’s Applied History Project Faculty Working Group for dinner. Faust, who regularly cites her training in history as her “professional identity” and her “field of scholarly inquiry for many decades,” asks herself a recurrent question: “I don’t know if I became a historian because I think the way I do, or if I think the way I do because I became a historian.” Whatever the answer, Faust felt at home in a room full of historians at the dinner. She participated in order to learn about the Project’s mission—the explicit attempt to illuminate current choices and challenges by analyzing precedents and analogues—and to discuss the value of history in life and leadership more generally. Nearly a decade into her presidency, Faust continues to shine as a historian’s historian. Having written award-winning histories of Civil War-era America, she now applies historical insights to leading America’s oldest university. At the dinner, Faust reinforced the argument she set forth in her Morning Prayers from August: “History, like leadership, is about change, about understanding what makes change happen, about who embraces change and why, and about who resists it and how.” “When someone presents me a problem,” Faust said, “I want to know: Where did it come from? What’s the history of it? How did this originate? What’s the backstory?” Subscribing to the historian’s view that almost nothing is new under the sun, she said, “I always feel that a conflict in the present, an opportunity in the present, is so shaped by where it came from. I operate very much as a historian, recognizing that issues don’t just drop out of the sky.” The latest from Belfer See more Recommended citation Kapur, Arjun, "Harvard President Faust Links History and Leadership." _list.source: Belfer Center for Science and International Affairs, Harvard Kennedy School

result.main_content_en: In her address at Harvard's Morning Prayers and during a dinner with the Belfer Center's Applied History Project, President Drew Gilpin Faust reflected on the connection between historical training and leadership. She expressed surprise at being asked what being a historian had to do with leading Harvard, noting that history provides ideal preparation for leadership by fostering an understanding of change, its causes, and the dynamics of resistance and acceptance. Faust described history as her professional identity and scholarly foundation, emphasizing that her historical perspective shapes her approach to problem-solving: she consistently seeks origins, backstories, and precedents to understand contemporary challenges. She operates on the principle that present conflicts and opportunities are deeply shaped by their past, and that few issues emerge without historical roots. Through her decade-long presidency, Faust has applied historical insights to university leadership, arguing that history and leadership both revolve around understanding and navigating change.
result.main_content_zh: 哈佛大学校长德鲁·吉尔平·福斯特在晨祷仪式上与贝尔弗中心应用历史项目工作组的晚宴中，探讨了历史训练与领导力之间的关联。她对有人质疑历史学家身份与哈佛校长职责的相关性表示惊讶，认为历史学通过培养对变革、其动因以及抵抗与接纳动态的理解，为领导力提供了理想的准备。福斯特将历史视作她的专业身份和学术根基，强调她的历史观塑造了她解决问题的方式：她总是追溯起源、背景和先例，以理解当代挑战。她秉持的原则是，当前的冲突和机遇都深受其历史背景的影响，几乎没有问题是凭空出现的。在长达十年的校长任期中，福斯特将历史洞察应用于大学领导工作，主张历史与领导力都围绕着理解与驾驭变革展开。
